prompt: When scheduling a new patient appointment, the CMAA should collect:

options.text: Demographics, insurance info, chief reason for visit, and provider/time needs
Only the patient's favorite color
No callback number
Diagnosis codes invented without a provider

explanation: Complete intake reduces denials and no-shows; MAs/admins don't invent diagnoses.

prompt: A matrix/template in scheduling is used to:

options.text: Bill insurance automatically without claims
Block times for visit types, procedures, and provider preferences
Replace the need for patient names
Store PHI on public social media

explanation: Templates improve flow (physicals vs acute slots, catch-up time).

prompt: Double-booking may be appropriate when:

options.text: Used randomly without clinical judgment causing chronic backlog
Patients are never informed of delays
Two patients can be seen in overlapping slots planned for short visits/provider workflow
It replaces triage of urgent needs

explanation: Intentional overlap differs from chaotic overbooking; communicate wait times.

prompt: When a patient arrives late, the CMAA should:

options.text: Refuse all future care permanently without policy
Argue at the front desk loudly with PHI details
Skip checking them in entirely while they wait indefinitely without update
Follow office policy—possibly reschedule or fit in without disrupting critical care

explanation: Policies balance fairness and access; maintain professionalism/privacy.

prompt: Referral scheduling often requires verifying:

options.text: Insurance authorization/referral numbers and specialist network status
Only the referring doctor's hobbies
That no records are sent ever
Cash prices for the specialist without patient consent discussions when relevant finances apply—skip all verification

explanation: Missing auth causes denials; collect/send needed clinical records.

prompt: Wave scheduling means:

options.text: One patient per day only
Several patients scheduled at the same hour and seen in arrival/order workflow
No appointments—walk-ins only always
Random cancellation of all new patients

explanation: Wave/modified wave are classic office scheduling methods taught in admin programs.

prompt: An encounter form/superbill is used to:

options.text: Replace the medical record entirely
Serve as a controlled substance Rx pad always
Capture services/procedures/diagnoses for billing from the visit
Check out library books

explanation: Superbills feed claim creation; documentation must still support codes.

distractorExplanations.b: Not a DEA Rx form inherently.

prompt: When handling a phone triage-type call about chest pain, the CMAA should:

options.text: Schedule a visit in three months without escalation
Diagnose GERD confidently
Ignore because they are not clinical
Follow emergency protocols—advise calling 911/ER per script and escalate immediately

explanation: Admin staff use protocols; life-threat symptoms get emergency direction.

prompt: No-show policies should be:

options.text: Applied consistently, documented, and explained in patient communications
Used to publicly shame patients in the waiting room
Different for every staff mood
Hidden from patients entirely always

explanation: Clear policies reduce conflict and support continuity.

prompt: Cluster scheduling groups:

options.text: All emergency surgeries in the lobby
Similar appointments together (e.g., all physicals in a block)
No like appointments ever
Only billing calls at midnight without staffing

explanation: Clustering improves efficiency for rooms/equipment/staffing.

prompt: Wave scheduling is designed to:

options.text: Book only one patient per day
Schedule multiple patients at the same arrival time to improve flow, then space them
Eliminate check-in entirely
Force all visits to be walk-in only

explanation: Wave scheduling clusters arrivals to reduce idle provider time while managing wait.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Book only one patient per day
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Force all visits to be walk-in only

prompt: When verifying insurance before a visit, the CMAA should typically confirm:

options.text: Only the patient's favorite pharmacy
Eligibility, coverage, and referral/authorization requirements as applicable
The physician's personal NPI by guessing
Nothing if the patient has a card photo

explanation: Eligibility and auth checks reduce denials and surprise patient bills.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Only the patient's favorite pharmacy
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: The physician's personal NPI by guessing
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Nothing if the patient has a card photo

prompt: A no-show policy is most effective when it is:

options.text: Secret and never told to patients
Clearly communicated, consistently applied, and documented
Changed randomly each hour
Used to refuse emergencies illegally

explanation: Transparent, consistent no-show policies support operations and fairness.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Secret and never told to patients
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Used to refuse emergencies illegally

prompt: Which information is essential when creating a new patient demographic record?

options.text: Accurate legal name, DOB, contact, and insurance identifiers
Only a nickname
The CMAA's home address
The patient's social media passwords

explanation: Correct demographics underpin registration, billing, and clinical identification.

distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: The patient's social media passwords

prompt: If two patients arrive claiming the same appointment slot, the CMAA should:

options.text: Argue loudly in the waiting room
Review the schedule/documentation calmly and escalate per office protocol to resolve the conflict
Delete both charts
Charge both patients double automatically

explanation: De-escalate, verify records, and follow protocol; protect privacy while resolving errors.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Argue loudly in the waiting room
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Charge both patients double automatically

prompt: The legal health record is best described as:

options.text: Documentation of care used for clinical, legal, and billing purposes under retention rules
Only sticky notes never entered
The waiting room sign-in clipboard as the full chart
Social media messages exclusively

explanation: Entries should be timely, accurate, authenticated, and complete.

distractorExplanations.d: Social media not the legal record.

prompt: HIPAA allows PHI disclosure without authorization for:

options.text: Marketing sale of PHI without limits always
Treatment, payment, and healthcare operations (TPO) under the Privacy Rule
Public gossip
Employer curiosity without involvement

explanation: Minimum necessary applies to many payment/operations uses; treatment has nuances.

distractorExplanations.d: Employers generally need authorization/other legal basis.

prompt: A valid authorization to release records generally includes:

options.text: Only a verbal 'sure' from an unknown caller
Provider initials alone forever
Who, what, to whom, purpose, expiration, and signature/date elements
Blank unsigned forms filled later by staff creatively

explanation: Defective authorizations should be rejected/fixed before release.

prompt: Correcting an EHR error should follow policy such as:

options.text: Deleting the encounter entirely to hide the mistake
Altering another clinician's note silently
Inserting false times
Addendum/correction that preserves the original entry (no obliteration)

explanation: Audit trails track changes; honesty protects legal integrity.

prompt: ROI (release of information) turnaround should:

options.text: Follow federal/state timelines and facility procedures with tracking logs
Ignore urgent care continuum needs always
Release entire charts to anyone paying cash without checks
Mail records to unverified addresses casually

explanation: Verify identity/authority; account for sensitive records (psych/HIV/substance) special rules.

prompt: The Notice of Privacy Practices (NPP) is provided to:

options.text: Only pharmaceutical sales reps
Patients to explain uses of PHI and patient rights, with acknowledgment efforts
No one—it's optional wallpaper
Competitors for marketing lists

explanation: Covered entities must make NPP available and retain acknowledgments when required.

distractorExplanations.a: Not for drug reps as the patient duty.

prompt: Minimum necessary standard means:

options.text: Everyone in the building may read all charts
Reception must memorize full psychiatric notes
Limit PHI access/use/disclosure to the least needed for the purpose (with treatment exceptions nuances)
Billers need full psychotherapy process notes always

explanation: Role-based access implements minimum necessary.

distractorExplanations.d: Specially protected notes have extra limits.

prompt: Retention of adult medical records is:

options.text: One week maximum always nationwide
Destroyed immediately after each visit always
Kept forever in the front desk drawer unlocked with no policy
Governed by state law and payer/accreditation policies (often years beyond last visit)

explanation: Know that retention varies; follow the strictest applicable rule/policy.

prompt: A personal representative for an adult patient is typically:

options.text: Someone with legal authority (e.g., healthcare power of attorney) as defined by law
Any coworker
A journalist
Anyone who knows the patient's birthday

explanation: Verify authority before discussing PHI or obtaining authorizations.

prompt: Faxing PHI safely includes:

options.text: Faxing to wrong numbers without verification routinely
Verifying numbers, using cover sheets, and placing machines in secure areas
Posting faxes on public boards
Disabling transmission audits always

explanation: Misdirected faxes are common breaches—confirm before send.

prompt: Under HIPAA, protected health information (PHI) includes:

options.text: Only hospital cafeteria menus
Individually identifiable health information in any form
Weather reports
Publicly posted clinic hours with no patient data

explanation: PHI is health information that can identify an individual.

distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Publicly posted clinic hours with no patient data

prompt: When releasing records to a third party, the CMAA generally needs:

options.text: A verbal request from any caller
A valid patient authorization (or another permitted disclosure) covering the request
Only the requester's email address
Permission from a pharmaceutical sales rep

explanation: Most non-TPO disclosures require proper authorization or another HIPAA pathway.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: A verbal request from any caller
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Only the requester's email address
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Permission from a pharmaceutical sales rep

prompt: How should a paper chart error typically be corrected?

options.text: Erase thoroughly until unreadable
Draw a single line through the error, initial/date, and enter the correction per policy (no obliteration)
Use white-out to hide the original
Remove the page from the chart

explanation: Corrections must remain legible and auditable.

distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Use white-out to hide the original
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Remove the page from the chart

prompt: Retention of medical records should follow:

options.text: Whatever is convenient that week
State law, payer, and facility retention policies
Only social media trends
Immediate shredding after each visit always

explanation: Retention periods are legally and organizationally defined.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Whatever is convenient that week
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Immediate shredding after each visit always

prompt: Role-based access controls in an EHR help ensure:

options.text: Every employee can see all celebrity charts
Workforce members access only the PHI needed for their job functions
Passwords are shared at the front desk
Audit logs are disabled

explanation: Minimum necessary access reduces privacy risk.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Every employee can see all celebrity charts
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Passwords are shared at the front desk

prompt: CPT codes primarily describe:

options.text: Procedures and services performed
Diagnoses only
Drug NDCs only
Place of service only

explanation: ICD-10-CM = diagnoses; CPT/HCPCS = services/supplies.

prompt: A claim denial for 'timely filing' means:

options.text: The patient was on time for the visit
The claim was submitted after the payer's deadline
The provider's license expired that day always as the only meaning
CPT and ICD mismatched only

explanation: Track filing limits; appeal/correct within payer rules.

distractorExplanations.d: Code mismatch is another denial reason.

prompt: Coordination of benefits (COB) determines:

options.text: Which exam room to use
The provider's lunch break
Which insurance pays first when a patient has multiple coverages
Whether SOAP notes are required

explanation: Primary vs secondary billing order prevents overpayment.

prompt: An Advanced Beneficiary Notice (ABN) relates to:

options.text: Always guaranteeing Medicaid payment
Workers' comp exclusive remedy only
Dental cleanings exclusively worldwide
Medicare situations where a service may not be covered and patient may be financially liable

explanation: Proper ABN issuance matters before the service when required.

prompt: ICD-10-CM codes should be selected based on:

options.text: Provider documentation to the highest specificity supported
Whichever code pays most regardless of documentation
Patient preference for scary-sounding diseases
Random codebook pages

explanation: Codes must be supported; upcoding is fraud risk.

prompt: A clean claim is one that:

options.text: Is printed on green paper only
Has complete/accurate data enabling payer processing without pending for missing info
Contains no diagnosis
Uses nickname instead of legal name always

explanation: Front-desk data quality (subscriber ID, DOB, NPI, POS) drives clean claims.

prompt: Day sheet/accounts receivable concepts help the office track:

options.text: Only clinical vitals
Sterilizer BI results
Charges, payments, adjustments, and outstanding balances
Employee blood titers only

explanation: Posting accuracy prevents billing disputes.

prompt: Modifier 25 is used when:

options.text: Bilateral surgery
Assistant surgeon
Repeat lab test only
A significant, separately identifiable E/M is billed same day as a procedure

explanation: Documentation must support separateness from the procedure's inherent E/M.

prompt: Patient statements should:

options.text: Clearly show dates of service, charges, payments, and amount due with appeal/question contacts
Include other patients' balances 'for comparison'
Hide the provider NPI forever when required on claims differently—confuse patients intentionally
Threaten violence

explanation: Transparent billing is ethical and often regulated by state rules.

prompt: Preauthorization differs from precertification discussions in that both generally mean:

options.text: A guarantee of payment in all cases without meeting terms
Payer approval processes required before certain services for coverage
Only a clinical consent form
An OSHA requirement

explanation: Auth doesn't always guarantee final payment if eligibility/medical necessity fails.

prompt: A deductible is:

options.text: The fixed amount paid at every visit regardless of plan design
The amount the patient must pay before the plan begins paying covered services (per plan rules)
The provider's tax ID
A type of CPT modifier

explanation: Deductibles are patient responsibility before insurance payment applies (subject to plan design).

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: The fixed amount paid at every visit regardless of plan design
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: A type of CPT modifier

prompt: Which claim form is commonly used for professional (physician) billing?

options.text: UB-04 only
SDS sheet
DEA Form 222 for all visits

explanation: CMS-1500 is the standard professional claim; UB-04 is institutional.

distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: DEA Form 222 for all visits

prompt: Coordination of benefits (COB) is needed when:

options.text: A patient has no insurance
A patient has more than one insurance plan that may cover the same services
Only cash-pay visits occur
The clinic buys new printers

explanation: COB determines primary vs secondary payment order.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: A patient has no insurance
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: The clinic buys new printers

prompt: An encounter form / superbill typically lists:

options.text: Only employee vacation days
Common diagnoses and procedures to support charge capture for a visit
Pharmacy inventory par levels
MRI images

explanation: Superbills help capture services/diagnoses for billing.

prompt: If a claim is denied for missing information, the CMAA should generally:

options.text: Resubmit the identical claim without review
Review the denial reason, correct the claim/documentation, and resubmit or appeal as appropriate
Bill the patient triple automatically
Delete the patient's chart

explanation: Denial management requires root-cause correction and proper resubmission/appeal.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Resubmit the identical claim without review
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Bill the patient triple automatically

prompt: Active listening includes:

options.text: Paraphrasing, clarifying, and attending to verbal/nonverbal cues
Interrupting with solutions immediately always
Looking only at the computer never the patient
Finishing every sentence for the patient

explanation: Builds rapport and reduces errors in intake.

prompt: If a patient is hostile, the CMAA should:

options.text: Match their insults
Stay calm, set boundaries, ensure safety, and involve supervisor/security as needed
Reveal another patient's information to distract them
Lock them in a room alone without protocol

explanation: De-escalation and safety first; document incidents.

prompt: Empathy differs from sympathy in that empathy:

options.text: Means diagnosing their disease
Requires sharing PHI of similar patients
Seeks to understand the patient's feelings/perspective without necessarily sharing the same emotion
Is unprofessional always

explanation: Empathic statements validate experience; remain professional boundaries.

prompt: Ethics of confidentiality means:

options.text: Sharing charts with friends for learning without approval
Posting de-identified funny cases that are actually identifiable
Selling celebrity visit rumors
Not discussing patient information with unauthorized persons

explanation: Even 'interesting cases' need authorization or proper de-identification standards.

prompt: When cultural/language barriers exist, best practice is:

options.text: Use qualified interpreters rather than minor children when possible
Speak louder English only
Guess medical terms in mime for consent
Skip informed intake questions

explanation: Language access improves safety and compliance with requirements.

prompt: Professional boundaries include:

options.text: Becoming business partners with every patient casually
Avoiding dual relationships that exploit patients and keeping communications appropriate
Dating patients while they are under active care as best practice
Accepting lavish gifts that influence scheduling unfairly

explanation: Follow office policies on gifts/relationships.

prompt: If you overhear coworkers gossiping about a patient's STI, you should:

options.text: Join in with more details
Post it anonymously online
Remind them of privacy rules and report per policy if needed
Ignore repeated systemic violations forever

explanation: Everyone is responsible for the privacy culture.

prompt: Informed consent discussions are primarily the responsibility of:

options.text: The front desk diagnosing risks independently
The billing company alone
A pharmaceutical sales rep
The provider performing the procedure, with CMAA supporting forms/logistics

explanation: Admin staff witness/organize consents but don't replace clinician disclosure.

distractorExplanations.a: Front desk doesn't give clinical informed consent alone.

prompt: Customer service recovery after a registration error should include:

options.text: Apology, correction, and prevention steps without blaming the patient unfairly
Hiding the error in claims
Charging an 'embarrassment fee'
Yelling that the patient filled forms wrong only

explanation: Service recovery rebuilds trust and reduces complaints/reviews fallout.

prompt: Workplace harassment should be:

options.text: Accepted as 'just joking' always
Reported per HR policy; patients and staff deserve a respectful environment
Retaliated against reporters
Filmed and posted publicly with PHI

explanation: Follow zero-tolerance procedures; protect reporters from retaliation.

prompt: When leaving a telephone message for a patient, the CMAA should:

options.text: Announce the full diagnosis and lab values on voicemail
Leave minimal necessary information per policy to protect privacy
Discuss results with whoever answers the phone
Post the message on the clinic Facebook page

explanation: Messages should avoid unnecessary PHI disclosure.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Announce the full diagnosis and lab values on voicemail
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Discuss results with whoever answers the phone
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Post the message on the clinic Facebook page

prompt: Empathy in patient communication means:

options.text: Agreeing that policies never apply
Acknowledging feelings while remaining professional and solution-focused
Sharing other patients' stories in detail
Avoiding eye contact and walking away

explanation: Empathy validates emotion without abandoning professional boundaries.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Agreeing that policies never apply
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Sharing other patients' stories in detail
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Avoiding eye contact and walking away

prompt: A conflict of interest example for a CMAA is:

options.text: Following the attendance policy
Accepting kickbacks to refer patients to a specific vendor
Clocking out on time
Attending HIPAA training

explanation: Kickbacks and biased referrals violate ethics and often law (e.g., anti-kickback rules).

prompt: Which is appropriate when a language barrier exists?

options.text: Rely on a minor child to interpret complex medical information routinely
Use a qualified interpreter/service per facility policy
Speak louder in English only
Skip informed forms entirely

explanation: Qualified interpreters support accurate, ethical communication.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Rely on a minor child to interpret complex medical information routinely
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Speak louder in English only

prompt: Professional boundaries with patients include:

options.text: Becoming financially entangled with patients for personal gain
Maintaining a therapeutic, respectful relationship without exploitation
Asking patients for large personal loans
Dating patients while accessing their charts for curiosity

explanation: Administrative staff must maintain professional, non-exploitative boundaries.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Becoming financially entangled with patients for personal gain
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Asking patients for large personal loans
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Dating patients while accessing their charts for curiosity
